7OvZhQIAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Architecture, Compiler, GPU Architecture, GPGPU Performance Modeling, Heterogeneous Computing (CPU–GPU), Memory Systems and 3D-Stacked DRAM, Prefetching and Memory Latency Tolerance, Processing-in-Memory (PIM) / Near-Memory Computing, Graph Analytics on GPUs, Edge/IoT Deep Neural Network Inference
7OvZhQIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Hyesoon Kim is a **Professor** in the School of Computer Science at **Georgia Tech**, where she leads a research group that spans *computer architecture*, *compilers*, and systems research. Her work centers on modeling, analyzing, and optimizing modern high-performance and heterogeneous computing platforms with a practical emphasis on GPUs, CPUs+GPUs, and memory-centric accelerators. Her group develops tools and techniques that expose and mitigate performance and energy bottlenecks by combining principled analytical models, compiler support, and system-level mechanisms to address <u>memory-level parallelism</u>, <u>thread-level parallelism</u>, and <u>processing-in-memory</u> challenges.

---

## Research Areas
Hyesoon Kim's research covers a broad spectrum in **computer architecture** and **compilers**, with particular depth in GPU/system performance modeling, heterogeneous-multicore mapping, memory-system management, and accelerator-aware compilation. Her highly-cited work on GPU performance (e.g., the 2009 analytical GPU model and 2010 integrated GPU power/performance model) provides a foundation for understanding how <u>memory-level and thread-level parallelism</u> interact on many-core devices. She has advanced techniques for scheduling and mapping on *heterogeneous multiprocessors* (notably Qilin), strategies for cache and prefetch management across many threads (multiple prefetching and TLP-aware cache policies), and practical simulation infrastructure (MacSim) for evaluating CPU–GPU systems. More recently, her group has driven research in security (SGX branch-shadowing side channels), near-memory and processing-in-memory (PIM) offloading with compiler assistance (Cairo) and hardware/software co-design for graph analytics (GraphPIM, GraphBIG), as well as studies on deploying deep neural networks at the edge and minimizing the costs of unified GPU memory and large-scale graph traversal. Across these topics she blends *analytic modeling*, *profiling-driven optimization*, and *compiler/runtime support* to produce reproducible tools and impactful system insights.

---

## Notable Works
- "An analytical model for a GPU architecture with memory-level and thread-level parallelism awareness" (2009) — a foundational analytical framework that quantifies how <u>MLP</u> and <u>TLP</u> shape GPU performance and guides both architecture and software optimizations for many-core GPUs.  
- "Inferring fine-grained control flow inside {SGX} enclaves with branch shadowing" (2017) — a high-impact security study that demonstrates a practical microarchitectural side channel against Intel SGX, influencing subsequent work on enclave defenses and microarchitectural attack mitigations.  
- "Qilin: exploiting parallelism on heterogeneous multiprocessors with adaptive mapping" (2009) — a seminal systems paper that proposes adaptive mapping strategies to exploit diverse compute units in heterogeneous processors, advancing automated hardware/software co-optimization for energy- and performance-efficient execution.

---

## Academic Background
Hyesoon Kim is a long-standing leader in architecture and compiler research based at **Georgia Tech**, with a publication portfolio that has accrued over 10,000 citations and spans more than a decade of influential contributions. Her research program combines analytic modeling, tool and simulator development (e.g., MacSim), and compiler/runtime mechanisms to address practical problems in performance, power, security, and programmability for modern accelerators. She has mentored numerous Ph.D. students and collaborators, produced multiple widely used artifacts and evaluation frameworks, and regularly publishes in top-tier venues in architecture, systems, and programming languages. Her work has attracted support and collaboration from both academic and industry partners, and she is recognized as a leader shaping how GPU and heterogeneous systems are analyzed, programmed, and secured.

7P-gZioAAAAJ.keywords: Systems for ML, soft real-time ML, LLM inference, Serverless computing, Stateful FaaS, Distributed cluster scheduling, GPU inference optimization, Latency SLOs and tail latency, Heterogeneous cloud resource management, Long-context LLM inference
7P-gZioAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Alexey Tumanov is an **Associate Professor** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads a research group focused on building practical, high-performance systems for machine learning. His lab sits at the intersection of **distributed systems** and **machine learning systems**, concentrating on designing infrastructure that enables *soft real-time ML* and scalable <u>LLM inference</u>. Tumanov's work spans end-to-end pipeline orchestration, adaptive scheduling, and inference-serving optimizations that prioritize both *latency SLOs* and *high utilization* across heterogeneous hardware.

---

## Research Areas
Tumanov's research addresses core challenges in deploying and operating ML at scale. He has contributed foundational work on distributed frameworks for AI (e.g., the design principles behind **Ray**) and on characterizing real-world cloud heterogeneity through large-scale trace analysis, which informed scheduler design for mixed workloads. He has advanced the state of the art in *serverless ML* and stateful FaaS (e.g., **Cloudburst**, critiques and extensions of first-generation serverless platforms), and developed systems for latency-aware ML serving and pipeline composition (e.g., **Inferline**). More recently his focus has shifted toward the unique systems problems of large language models — including end-to-end inference evaluation (**Etalon**), simulation for LLM inference (**Vidur**), and practical throughput–latency tradeoffs in decoding (**Sarathi-Serve**, work on KV cache compression and long-context serving like **Medha** and **RocketKV**). Across these topics he emphasizes heterogeneous-resource scheduling (e.g., **TetriSched**, **Proteus**, **3sigma**) and techniques for improving GPU utilization and tail-latency guarantees in production ML services.

---

## Notable Works
- Ray: A distributed framework for emerging AI applications (2018) — a widely-cited systems framework that shaped how modern interactive and reinforcement-learning applications are built and deployed.
- Heterogeneity and dynamicity of clouds at scale: Google trace analysis (2012) — a seminal empirical study revealing cluster heterogeneity and workload dynamics that influenced resource management and scheduler research.
- Taming Throughput-Latency tradeoff in LLM inference with Sarathi-Serve (2024) — a focused contribution on decoding-phase optimization for large language models that balances *prefill* and *decode* phases to improve end-to-end latency and GPU efficiency.

---

## Academic Background
Alexey Tumanov has been an active researcher in distributed systems and ML systems for well over a decade, with publication records dating back to the late 2000s and a total citation count exceeding **8,700**. As an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech he combines academic leadership with extensive collaborative work alongside industry and cloud-provider partners, contributing to both open-source frameworks and production-oriented evaluation tools. His portfolio demonstrates sustained impact across topics including cluster scheduling, serverless computing, ML workflow orchestration, and the rapidly evolving landscape of LLM inference. He has mentored students and collaborators through multiple system-building projects and continues to serve the community through conference program roles, workshops, and cross-disciplinary collaborations that translate systems research into deployable ML infrastructure.

_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.keywords: human-computer interaction, information visualization, visual analytics, sensemaking, analytic provenance, semantic interaction, interactive machine learning, mixed-initiative visual analytics, cognitive bias mitigation, natural language interfaces
_ZIAy3cAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Alex Endert is an **Associate Professor** in the College of Computing at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group within the Georgia Tech Visual Analytics Lab (see http://va.gatech.edu/endert). His work centers on **visual analytics**, *human–computer interaction*, and **information visualization**, with a particular emphasis on enabling and studying analytic reasoning through interactive systems. Across his research he investigates how people and algorithms can collaborate during sensemaking, focusing on <u>interactive model steering</u>, <u>provenance-aware analysis</u>, and <u>display and interaction designs for complex analytic workspaces</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Endert’s scholarship spans core topics in **visual analytics** and **information visualization**, often bridging algorithmic models and human interaction. He has explored how physical and virtual workspaces support cognition (e.g., large, high-resolution displays and spatial organization in “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking”), how interaction can implicitly steer models and represent users’ analytical reasoning (e.g., the family of *semantic interaction* papers such as “Semantic interaction for visual text analytics” and “Semantic interaction for sensemaking”), and how provenance and analytic history can be characterized and used to improve reproducibility and interpretability (see “Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis”). More recently, his work examines the integration of **machine learning** into interactive visual analytics pipelines (“The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics”), methods for surfacing automated insights and facts to aid interpretation, and mechanisms to detect or mitigate human cognitive biases during interactive analysis. Across these topics he combines user studies, interaction design, and algorithmic innovations to support <u>human-centered, mixed-initiative</u> analytic workflows.

## Notable Works
- “Space to think: large high-resolution displays for sensemaking” (2010) — seminal empirical and design work demonstrating how **large, high-resolution displays** and spatial externalization support complex sensemaking tasks, influencing subsequent research on display-based workspaces and co-located collaboration.  
- “Characterizing provenance in visualization and data analysis: an organizational framework of provenance types and purposes” (2015) — a foundational framework that organizes how provenance has been conceptualized and used in visualization and analytics, shaping how systems track, present, and leverage analytic history.  
- “The state of the art in integrating machine learning into visual analytics” (2017) — a comprehensive review and synthesis that articulates principles, challenges, and directions for coupling **machine learning** with interactive visualization to promote effective human–machine analytic partnerships.

## Academic Background
Alex Endert is an established researcher in the HCI and visualization communities and an Associate Professor at Georgia Tech’s College of Computing, where he directs work in the Visual Analytics Lab. His publication record includes numerous highly cited conference and journal papers (total citations: **7,616**) that appear in leading venues of **visualization**, **HCI**, and **visual analytics**. He regularly contributes to community directions on topics such as mixed-initiative analysis, interactive machine learning, analytic provenance, and bias-aware visualization. Endert mentors graduate students and collaborates widely across academia, industry, and government, producing both foundational theory and prototype systems that have advanced understanding of how interactive visual interfaces can couple human cognition with computational models.
